content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

SACRAMENTO

770 L STREET, SUITE 800
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752

591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000
MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941

SAN FRANCISCO

650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108

TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800

TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800

March 13, 1991

Blanca Breeze, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Ms. Breeze:

    I am writing to request written confirmation of advice you
provided me on the telephone on March 8, 1991.

    I had made an inquiry regarding gifts to public officials
from multiple donors under the new gift limit of section 89504.
We also discussed how the public official and lobbyist
employer/donor would disclose such gifts. My understanding of
your advice is summarized below.

    When a public official receives a gift from more than one
person, only that portion of the gift actually paid by each donor
will count toward the $250 annual gift limit of section 89504 for
that donor. However, the public official will disclose the total
value of the gift if it exceeds $50 on his or her Statement of
Economic Interest, but not identify any multiple donor whose
share is less than $50 (as long as that donor's cumulative total
in the calendar year does not exceed $50). (2 CCR § 18726.6.)
Any donor who is a Lobbyist Employer will disclose its total
"activity expense," its portion of the cost of the gift and the
total benefit to the public official, and the total cost of the
joint expenditure.

    Assume for instance that two Lobbyist Employers take state
official Jane Smith to dinner at Frank Fat's and they split the

¹Charles Bell of our office requested similar advice in his
February 6, 1991 letter to Jeanne Pritchard on behalf of the
California Legislative Forum. His letter concerned an
organization that invites state and federal officials to an event
in Washington, D.C. after receiving payments from various
sponsors. This present letter simply requests limitation and
reporting advice for multiple donors and public official
recipients.

Blanca Breeze, Esq.
March 13, 1991
Page 2

total cost of $220 equally. If the cost of Smith's meal is $80,
then the meal is reported as follows:

(a) Smith will have received a gift of $40 from each
Lobbyist Employer for purposes of calculating the $250 annual
gift limit;

(b) Smith will disclose the $80 gift as coming from
two donors who each paid less than $50 (see example attached);²
and

(c) Each Lobbyist Employer will disclose a $110
activity expense, the cost of the official's meal ($80), the
total meal cost ($220), and the filer's share for limits purposes
($40) (see example attached).

In short, the gift will be reported in the same manner as it
was before January 1, 1991, with each donor's gift identified for
purposes of the limitations.

If this letter does not accurately portray the advice you
rendered, please call me immediately.

Thank you for your time and effort.

Very truly yours,

James R. Sutton

JRS/sfr

Enclosures

5909.01


²As long as the official does not receive other gifts from
the same donors over the course of the calendar year putting
their gift totals over $50, the exact portion paid by each donor
need not be disclosed.

I had made an inquiry regarding gifts to public officials from multiple donors under the new gift limit of section 89504. We also discussed how the public official and lobbyist employer/donor would disclose such gifts. My understanding of your advice is summarized below. When a public official receives a gift from more than one person, only that portion of the gift actually paid by each donor will count toward the $250 annual gift limit of section 89504 for that donor. However, the public official will disclose the total value of the gift if it exceeds $50 on his or her Statement of Economic Interest, but not identify any multiple donor whose share is less than $50 (as long as that donor's cumulative total in the calendar year does not exceed $50). (2 CCR § 18726.6.) In short, the gift will be reported in the same manner as it was before January 1, 1991, with each donor's gift identified for purposes of the limitations.
